title-group.article-title: Developing a minimum dataset for a national patient registry on Long COVID in Canada: a Delphi consensus-based study

contrib-group.aff: 2Research, Saskatchewan Health Authority, Saskatoon, Saskatchewan, Canada
3Research Department, Saskatchewan Health Authority, Regina, Saskatchewan, Canada
4Saskatchewan Health Quality Council, Saskatoon, Saskatchewan, Canada
5Community Health & Epidemiology, University of Saskatchewan College of Medicine, Saskatoon, Saskatchewan, Canada
9Department of Family Medicine, University of Alberta, Edmonton, Alberta, Canada
10Institute for Clinical Evaluative Sciences, Toronto, Ontario, Canada
11Rady Faculty of Health Sciences, University of Manitoba, Winnipeg, Manitoba, Canada
12Department of Physical Therapy, The University of British Columbia Faculty of Medicine, Vancouver, British Columbia, Canada
13School of Rehabilitation Science, McMaster University, Hamilton, Ontario, Canada
15Community Health and Epidemiology, University of Saskatchewan, Saskatoon, Saskatchewan, Canada

fn.p: Supplemental material This content has been supplied by the author(s). It has not been vetted by BMJ Publishing Group Limited (BMJ) and may not have been peer-reviewed. Any opinions or recommendations discussed are solely those of the author(s) and are not endorsed by BMJ. BMJ disclaims all liability and responsibility arising from any reliance placed on the content. Where the content includes any translated material, BMJ does not warrant the accuracy and reliability of the translations (including but not limited to local regulations, clinical guidelines, terminology, drug names and drug dosages), and is not responsible for any error and/or omissions arising from translation and adaptation or otherwise.

GG is a member of the Long COVID Web and has received funding to support this study. In-kind support was received from the Saskatchewan Health Authority, Saskatchewan Centre for Patient-Oriented Research, Saskatchewan Health Quality Council, University of Saskatchewan and SPOR Support Unit Council. The members of the Delphi Steering and Advisory Groups are members of the Long COVID Web. All other authors have no competing interests to declare.

license.license-p: This is an open access article distributed in accordance with the Creative Commons Attribution Non Commercial (CC BY-NC 4.0) license, which permits others to distribute, remix, adapt, build upon this work non-commercially, and license their derivative works on different terms, provided the original work is properly cited, appropriate credit is given, any changes made indicated, and the use is non-commercial. See: https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/4.0/.

sec.title: Objectives
sec.p: To develop survey items for a national patient registry on Long COVID using a modified Delphi process.

sec.title: Design
sec.p: This study was based on a modified Delphi process involving three rounds of anonymous, online surveys to develop consensus on and prioritise survey elements to be included in a minimum dataset for use in a national patient registry in Canada. Initial Long COVID items were identified through an environmental scan of the literature.

sec.title: Setting
sec.p: This study focused on healthcare systems in Canada and was conducted online.

sec.title: Participants
sec.p: A panel of 52 experts (patients, caregivers, clinicians and researchers) participated in all three rounds of the online survey. These participants were recruited through the Long COVID Web network and word of mouth.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: In total, 243 survey elements related to care, quality of life and symptoms were included in round 1 of the survey. 200 reached consensus and moved to round 2 with two additional elements being developed based on open-ended responses. In round 2, participants ranked these survey elements and 34 advanced. In round 3, 33 survey elements met the threshold of consensus with one added a priori. The 33 survey elements were then used to develop a Long COVID minimum dataset, which consists of 48 items.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: The findings affirm broad consensus for collecting data related to fatigue, post-exertional malaise, cardiovascular issues, respiratory problems and cognitive issues. This highlighted the desire for quality-of-life indicators and information related to care utilisation, quality and access.

caption.title: STRENGTHS AND LIMITATIONS OF THIS STUDY

list-item.p: The minimum dataset includes items on quality of care and barriers and facilitators to care access, which have implications for Canadian healthcare systems and distinguish this study from previous international consensus studies.
The low attrition rate supported the interpretability of the findings.
The panel’s representativeness was limited with respect to gender, ethnicity and healthcare system decision-makers.
The study did not employ focus group discussions between rounds, potentially reducing the number of new ideas or conceptual adaptations.
The study did not meet its target sample size of 100, though it still exceeded the mean and median panel sizes reported in most Delphi studies.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: In Canada, a recent study revealed that one in nine adults previously infected with COVID-19 had experienced long-term symptoms, and as of June 2023, most of these people continued to experience long-term symptoms.1 This condition, now commonly referred to as Long COVID (LC) or post-COVID-19 condition, is defined by Soriano et al,2 as “occurring in individuals with a history of probable or confirmed SARS CoV-2 infection, usually three months from the onset of COVID-19 with symptoms that last for at least two months and cannot be explained by an alternative diagnosis. Common symptoms include fatigue, shortness of breath, cognitive dysfunction but also others and generally have an impact on everyday functioning. Symptoms may be new onset following initial recovery from an acute COVID-19 episode or persist from the initial illness. Symptoms may also fluctuate or relapse over time.” This clinical definition originated from the WHO-led Delphi consensus process. So far, over 200 documented symptoms have been reported to date by individuals suffering from LC.3 The most common symptoms include fatigue, shortness of breath, muscle pain, joint pain, headache, cough, chest pain, altered smell, altered taste, diarrhoea, cognitive impairment, memory loss, anxiety and sleep disorders,4 with long-term effects being attributed towards the multi-systemic nature of the disease. In time, as patients continue to report on their experiences and our insight into the condition develops further, this definition will also evolve to reflect our new level of understanding.

However, given the novelty and breadth of emerging evidence, there is a pertinent and ongoing need to develop a systematic, standardised and uniform method for collecting and sharing data that describes disease symptomatology, treatments and outcomes. Recent work has prioritised the call to develop core outcomes and core outcome measurement sets as consensus-based parameters for LC.57 In brief, an outcome or domain refers to a concept of health that can be measured. A core outcome set is defined as a minimum set of outcomes that should be measured in clinical trials for a specific disease within a specific setting, and an outcome measurement set refers to how a set of outcomes are measured using instruments such as survey questionnaires, performance-based tests or laboratory measurements.8

Notable among these efforts toward systematic LC data collection is a study by Munblit et al5 who convened a diverse panel of 1535 participants from 71 countries in a Delphi process to develop a core outcome set for LC in adults, resulting in 11 outcomes within three domains with an additional outcome added a priori. Gorst et al6 used the Delphi approach to suggest specific validated measurement instruments to reliably capture the LC outcomes developed by Munblit et al.5 This study reduced the number of core outcome measurement instruments identified for each of the 12 LC core outcomes from 319 to 19; however, only three instruments for three outcomes reached the threshold of consensus (those measuring survival, recovery and respiratory functioning, symptoms and conditions). Ejalonibu et al7 evaluated several patient-reported outcome measures developed for LC according to their psychometric properties and the dimensions measured within each. By comparing these tools in conjunction with guidance from patients with LC, they were able to recommend a combination of condition-specific and symptom-specific measurement instruments that could be used to evaluate common LC outcomes.

Taken together, these works support global collaborative efforts to standardise data collection at an international level and make it possible for researchers to build on each other’s work to collectively progress LC research and support the identification of effective treatments. Moreover, these studies were conducted using diverse expert panels, lending them broader social relevance by including perspectives of, for instance, clinicians, caregivers and patients, while also improving the clinical relevance, psychometric rigour and generalisability of their findings.

Despite these strides in identifying and measuring core outcomes, the requirements for a national registry merit further investigation, due to two reasons. First, tools recommended to date do not include items related to healthcare experiences. Both Gorst et al6 and Ejalonibu et al7 focus on outcomes, with Gorst et al6 including a subset of questions examining impacts on daily life. Second, recommended tools encompass too many items for use in a national registry as opposed to a research study. Ejalonibu et al7 recommend four tools that range between 4 and 123 items and that each require between 5 and 30 min to administer. Gorst et al’s6 study establishes consensus on three single-item scales (for three core outcomes) but also recommends 16 instruments for nine core outcomes, with the instruments ranging from 1 to 40 items and from 1 to 10 min (at minimum) to complete. For the purposes of creating a national patient registry, our intent was to develop a minimum dataset (MDS)—“a coherent set of data elements which should be collected for specific categories or domains of healthcare.”9 For our purposes, an MDS would be defined as the least number of elements or items recommended to be included in a LC patient registry based on the current state of knowledge. The brevity of an MDS is an essential feature as LC questions are meant to be completed by people who may be struggling with declines in cognition, specifically, executive function, processing speed, memory and attention,1012 and busy healthcare professionals with competing priorities and demands.1315

sec.title: Aims and objectives
sec.p: In the context of the current study, and with the support of the Long COVID Web,16 Canadian patients, caregivers, researchers and healthcare professionals across Canada came together to develop a minimum dataset capable of (1) informing change in the Canadian healthcare context and (2) being administered quickly and effectively (ie, having no more than 50 individual questions). The resulting insights were gleaned over three rounds of survey questionnaires to establish a minimum dataset of 48 questions for use as an efficient tool for capturing salient data on LC. This dataset can be adapted for use in all provinces and territories across Canada to establish a national LC patient registry—a sustainable, sentinel data collection system for the condition.

sec.title: Study protocol
sec.p: The protocol for this study was developed using ACcurate COnsensus Reporting Document (ACCORD)17 18 and the Core Outcome Sets-STAndard for Development (COS-STAD) guidelines,19 and was previously published by our team.20 This paper was developed by our research team and was published in the first phase of our study. It highlights the steps taken to conduct the Delphi consensus process and construct the survey questions for an MDS to support the development of an LC patient registry and to inform future research. The round 1 survey is available for reference in our protocol manuscript.20

sec.title: Ethics approval and consent to participate
sec.p: The study began in May 2024 and ended in November 2024. Prospective participants were provided with an informed consent form over email and instructed to contact the study coordinator if they had any further questions or concerns. Consent was provided via private email. Sensitive information was shared separately over the phone when desired (eg, banking information to provide honoraria). Participants were anonymous to everyone except the study coordinator who required their information to track participation. Compensation was provided as $C100 for completing the first round, followed by $C50 after completion of the second round, and again after the third round. All data were de-identified before being analysed by members of the working team. The results of our study are reported using ACCORD, as mentioned above,17 18 and the Core Outcome Sets-STAndards for Reporting (COS-STAR) guidelines.19

sec.title: Patient and public involvement
sec.p: Three groups participated in the Delphi development and implementation: the steering, advisory and working groups. The make-up and activities of these groups are detailed in our protocol paper.20 Patient partners in the working group collaborated to provide input on the research proposal, ethics, survey design (including ease of use, feasibility and language), recruitment materials, study instructions and knowledge translation (eg, presentations and publications). Two people with lived experience of LC (neither of whom were involved in the steering, advisory or working groups) piloted the first survey round and provided feedback for ease of use, clarity and other potential barriers to completion. Pilot participants were not familiar with the study, and their survey responses were not used in the analysis. Members of the working, advisory and steering groups were permitted to participate in the Delphi (if they met study criteria), excluding those responsible for data analysis.

sec.title: Participant recruitment and engagement
sec.p: The target population for this study was persons with either lived experience (patients and caregivers) or professional experiences with LC (clinicians, researchers and policymakers). This was to ensure that the registry included information related to care, quality of life and symptoms that was meaningful and applicable to a broad range of knowledge users, while also maintaining relevance to the current state of knowledge. Recruitment was facilitated through electronic means via an online post and email message to the Long COVID Web membership list. In addition, recruitment efforts were extended through word of mouth and strategic emails to patient-oriented research groups and academic institutions across Canada. Interested parties emailed the study coordinator if they wished to participate.

The research coordinator collected screening information to determine eligibility and verify that the sampling of participants reflected a diverse cross-section of potential participants. Study participant inclusion criteria consisted of being 18 years of age or older, having internet access and being able to access the online survey web page, being able to access documents online and being able to read and understand English. Demographic information was gathered through a questionnaire sent to potential participants, including variables of age, gender, province of residence, residential community type, ethnicity, employment status, confirmation of COVID-19 diagnosis and nature of involvement with the Long COVID Web. Interested participants were selected to ensure variation within the sample with consideration for underrepresented groups; therefore, participants were prioritised to ensure a wide range of responses. While we did not employ formal stratified sampling with predetermined quotas, our project coordinator prioritised initial contact with individuals from subgroups that had lower response rates—such as those underrepresented by province, professional role or other characteristics. The target sample size (as noted in our protocol) was one hundred participants. This target was justified given previous ranges investigated in the literature and our wish to mitigate the risk of study attrition over consecutive rounds of survey questionnaires.2123 Only 52 participants completed all three rounds of the Delphi—a number that, though falling short of our target, still exceeded the mean and median numbers of respondents reported in most Delphi studies.22 23

The first round of online surveys began on 21 May 2024. Participants had 3 weeks (with a possible 1 week extension) to complete each survey round. They were informed that they could leave and return to the online survey as they wished during this time (to minimise cognitive burden). The option to receive a paper copy of the survey was also provided, though no participants opted for this. Participants who had not yet completed the survey were reminded to participate on a weekly basis, and on the day prior to each round’s closing.

sec.title: Consensus definition
sec.p: An important component of Delphi studies lies in the steps taken to define consensus, and there can be subsequent differing definitions depending on the context. The literature suggests that the rationale for defining consensus revolves around ensuring that an agreement or decision considers dissenting opinions, but reflects the collective viewpoint of participants.24 25

Concerning the specific cut-off criteria needed to define consensus, Hsu and Sandford24 state that consensus is achieved when either 80% of participant votes fall within two categories on a seven-point scale, or when 70% of participants provide ratings of 3 or higher on a four-point Likert scale with a median rating of 3.25 or greater. On the other hand, Diamond et al26 state that the most common definition for consensus is percent agreement, with 75% being the median threshold to define consensus among participants. Therefore, in our study, based on the benchmarks established by previous literature, we opted to utilise a minimum 70% agreement cut-off criterion to determine questions that were eligible for advancement into the second round of the study. The mean and SD of these datasets were calculated as measures of central tendency and spread.

To assess the level of consensus among participants, we utilised the IQR which is known for its statistical robustness. The IQR captures the middle 50% of participant responses and is less influenced by outliers, as compared with other measures of spread such as the range or SD. This characteristic makes the IQR particularly valuable in Delphi studies, where expert opinions can vary widely. A seminal article by von der Gracht27 indicates that an IQR of 1.5 or less suggests a strong consensus among the participants. Consequently, we used this parameter of an IQR ≤1.5 on a 1–5 scale in our study to establish consensus.

sec.title: Round 1
sec.p: The first round of the survey included 243 survey elements, and our purpose was to eliminate survey topics not essential to the minimum dataset. We anticipated this round would be the most cumbersome and time-consuming for participants, but we expected a large reduction in the number of topics going forward to future rounds. Topics were eliminated based on participant responses to the question, “How strongly do you agree or disagree that the following [survey element] should be part of a Long COVID minimum data set?” within the domains of care, quality of life and symptoms. Responses were recorded using a five-point Likert scale rating consisting of the following: (1) strongly disagree, (2) disagree, (3) neither agree nor disagree, (4) agree and (5) strongly agree. Responses were analysed quantitatively, and statistical techniques of mean, SD and IQR were used to quantify the level of agreement among participants.

sec.title: Round 2
sec.p: The second round included topics within care, quality of life and symptom foci that were prioritised by participants in the first round of the study. Participants were asked to select a predetermined number of survey elements from each of the care, quality of life and symptom themes, by providing ratings on the following question, “Which of the following [survey elements] should be part of a Long COVID minimum data set? Pick the [#] options that are most important to collect data on.” As discussed in previous sections, forced selection was introduced to identify priority survey elements across the various categories moving into the final rounds. This was determined between rounds one and two with a weighting calculation to balance the number of topics in each category.

Thus, we had to consider how the prioritised survey elements might be reassembled following round 3 to still arrive at an MDS of 50 questions with a reasonable distribution of questions across themes. It was decided by the advisory group that a weighting towards symptoms should be used (roughly 1:1:2 ratio between the care, quality of life and symptoms themes) in this round. The breakdown of this calculation is included in an online supplemental document ‘MDS Weighting Calculations’. The questions with the highest number of selections (based on mode tabulation) advanced into the third round.

sec.title: Round 3
sec.p: The purpose of the third round was to resolve any uncertainty on the survey topics that reached consensus in earlier rounds within the care, quality of life and symptom foci, along with the submitted open-ended feedback. We asked each participant to indicate the level of agreement on a scale of 1–5 to the question, “How strongly do you agree or disagree that the following [survey element] should be part of a Long COVID minimum data set?” where 1 indicated strong disagreement, and 5 indicated strong agreement for the questions that would make up the minimum dataset. The mean, median and SD of the dataset were calculated to determine consensus.

sec.title: Qualitative analysis of open-ended responses
sec.p: Open-ended participant responses were analysed following each round of the survey. Given the unique purposes of each round, the question wording shifted slightly, from requesting recommendations for topics related to care, quality of life and symptoms (round 1), to sharing details to explain priorities (round 2), to asking for details to explain final ratings (round 3). Three criteria (chosen by the working and advisory groups) were used to determine how to incorporate the open-ended question data. These criteria were determined after round 1 data had been collected and marked a modification to our study protocol.20

The criteria were as follows: first, we considered whether additional options or adaptations to survey elements were within the scope of the themes of care, quality of life and symptoms. Second, we identified whether a recommendation was put forward by two or more participants, using this as a threshold for considering a change to the question categories. This criterion has been used previously and mitigates the undue influence of outliers within the panel.28 Finally, in rounds 2 and 3, we considered whether the recommendation had been included in any preceding rounds. If it had, then we did not integrate it as a ‘new’ survey element, as we believed that the participants had already effectively chosen not to prioritise it. A minimum of two members of the working group reviewed all open-ended responses according to these criteria, and a minimum of two members (but as many as four members) of the working group evaluated possible ways to include the recommendations, which were then brought forward to the advisory group for approval. The decision to exclude open-ended feedback deemed out of scope was made a priori between members of the working group.

sec.title: Participant sampling and demographics
sec.p: Although 100 participants was the sample size target (to account for attrition), and 159 people expressed initial interest, only 52 participants completed all three rounds of the Delphi survey. Figure 1 details the flow of participants throughout the study, showing that the final participants were the same in each round. Altogether, the drop-out rate was deemed acceptable, with only 11 out of 63 participants dropping out after informed consent was obtained (82.5% retention).

caption.title: Flowchart of participants through the screening, recruitment and retention phases.

sec.p: Demographic details are presented in table 1. Of note is the representation across Canadian provinces and regions, and the robust representation of patients. Notable limitations are a skew towards women, a lack of racial diversity and the absence of policymakers or decision-makers.

caption.title: Characteristics of participants in the Delphi consensus process

fn.p: Participants were the same individuals between rounds
Participants were able to select more than one option.

sec.title: Round 1
sec.p: A total of 243 survey elements were presented for evaluation. Based on the statistical analysis, 200 elements were determined to meet the threshold of 70% agreement among participants and forty-three survey elements were cut. However, it is important to note that using the IQR measure ≤1, only 197 elements gained strong consensus from the participants (see online supplemental document ‘statistical analysis’).

From the round 1 open-ended feedback, two survey elements were added into round 2 in the social and emotional well-being section within quality of life: accepting a new way of life and grieving the life one had prior to LC. Accepting a new way of life refers to getting used to the new normal of planning and executing tasks and coming to terms with the changes in one’s life. Grieving the life one had prior to LC reflected a similar sentiment, while needing to emotionally process the substantial life change and accept that their occupation, abilities and pastimes may never be quite the same as they once were. Several participants noted in relation to the timeframes that focusing on the first infection was not relevant for them, as they developed LC after multiple infections. Consequently, the wording for the timeframes referring to infection points was modified to be ‘anytime after any COVID-19 infection’, ‘up to 3 months after any COVID-19 infection’ and ‘3 months or more after any COVID-19 infection until the present or until illness is resolved’.

sec.title: Round 2
sec.p: In round 2, 202 survey elements were included and based on participant feedback, a total of 34 survey elements were prioritised: 11 within care, 14 for symptoms and 9 for quality of life. However, in their open-ended responses, several participants indicated that more than 10 symptoms of LC should be included in the minimum dataset, as they believed not all the crucial symptoms would be captured within 10 survey elements, which could affect subsequent research and understanding of LC treatment and management. This was deliberated among the research team, and later on, the advisory group, and ultimately did not proceed as an adjustment due to the increase in categories being contrary to the round 2 objective of eliminating excess categories to create the most essential list of symptoms for the dataset.

Following analysis of the round 2 open-ended data, in the care section ‘description of healthcare services’, the quality of care category was expanded to include symptoms being recognised (or identified) as LC, effectively becoming ‘Quality of care (eg, symptoms being recognized (or identified) as LC, level of satisfaction, clarity of information provided, effectiveness of treatment, timeliness, empathy of healthcare professionals, taking illness seriously).’ This change was performed in accordance with two participants expressing the difficulty that may arise in having LC symptoms recognised and diagnosed by medical providers and the overall medical system.

sec.title: Round 3
sec.p: In the last round of the Delphi study, 33 survey elements met the threshold for consensus and progressed to the final stage of the process to develop the minimum dataset questions. Table 2 contains the final survey elements prioritised for the MDS. The results indicated that most of the questions received in this round had a high mean (>4) and a low SD (≤1.2) suggesting very strong agreement among participants that the selected elements should indeed comprise the minimum dataset. Figure 2 shows the distribution of levels of agreement among participants for these items.

caption.title: Summary of final survey elements prioritised

tr.td: Rehabilitation services (physical therapy, occupational therapy and speech therapy)
Specialty care for Long COVID (eg, specialised Long COVID clinic)
Primary care (routine healthcare—including vaccinations—provided by family doctors, family physicians, nurse practitioners and general practitioners)
Utilisation (eg, which healthcare services and professionals were used by patients)

Quality of care (eg, symptoms being recognised (or identified) as Long COVID, level of satisfaction, clarity of information provided, effectiveness of treatment, timeliness, empathy of healthcare professionals, taking illness seriously)

Barriers and facilitators to using healthcare services (eg, availability, ability to locate and find, ability to get a referral, out-of-pocket costs, time required to access, staying up to date with information on COVID-19, previous negative care experiences)
Support groups for people who have difficulties after COVID-19
Care—descriptions of informal caregivers and/or community supports
Utilisation (eg, which informal caregiver and community supports were used)
Barriers and facilitators to using informal caregivers or community supports (eg, availability, ability to locate and find, out-of-pocket costs, time required to access)
3 months or more after any COVID-19 infection until the present or until the illness resolved
Quality of life—social and emotional health
Being able to cope with life
Rating quality of life (eg, scale of 1 to 100)
Quality of life—timeframes for quality of life
3 months or more after any COVID-19 infection until the present or until the illness resolved
Sudden appearance of symptoms after previously tolerated (before the illness) casual physical activity (eg, walking)
Sudden appearance of symptoms after previously tolerated (before the illness) casual cognitive (mental) activity (eg, drawing)
Current symptom status (eg, persisting, worsening, fluctuating)
Impact of illness on daily activities over time (changes in ability to perform tasks, overall trajectory of functional abilities)
3 months or more after any COVID-19 infection until the present or until the illness resolved

caption.title: Distribution of participants’ level of agreement (on a scale from 1 to 5) regarding the appropriateness of questions included in the final minimum dataset. Most participants agreed or strongly agreed with the selection of questions.

sec.p: At this stage, no new recommendations were integrated into the Delphi study as there were no further rounds to evaluate them. Open-ended feedback received from participants that was not incorporated in each round of the study was summarised and can be viewed in the online supplemental document ‘additional recommendations from participants.

sec.title: Delphi survey reliability
sec.p: To assess the reliability of elements selected in each round of the Delphi survey, we utilised Cronbach’s α—a widely used measure for internal consistency which evaluates how closely the items within an instrument collate with each other.29 The value of the reliability coefficient can range between 0 and 1, with values over 0.7 deemed acceptable. However, the Cronbach’s α is sensitive to the number of items that are included in a measurement tool; therefore, generally, the greater the number of items in the tool, the higher Cronbach’s α will be.

In our study, we observed a Cronbach’s α of 0.903 for round 1, and 0.92 for round 3, which indicated a strong level of internal consistency among the questions. The high reliability suggests that the survey elements were measuring a coherent construct, and that participants’ responses were consistent across the rounds.

sec.title: Minimum dataset creation
sec.p: Based on the results of the third Delphi survey round, members of the working group transformed the concepts rated highly into survey questions on LC care, quality of life and symptoms with the appropriate timeframes for reporting this data. One member of the working team drafted these, drawing on (1) the structure and wording from the environmental scan where applicable and (2) survey design principles that would ensure ease of use (such as limiting matrix-style questions, providing consistent question framing and response options, sequencing questions to place similar topics together, etc). The drafted MDS questions were reviewed by five members of the working group (including two patient partners). Solutions to concerns and disagreements were addressed by three members of the working group, then brought to the larger working group and advisory group for approval and/or adjustments. ‘Utilization (eg, which informal caregiver and community supports were used)’ was added a priori in consultation with the advising committee. This item was included as a branching question to allow respondents to answer prioritised questions relating to the quality of care. The finalised minimum dataset is available as an online supplemental document ‘MDS items’.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: The objective of this study was to support the development of a national patient registry in Canada by creating a minimum dataset. The resulting dataset consists of 48 questions that address symptoms, quality of life and, uniquely, patient experience of healthcare. The study has been strengthened by the inclusion of patient partners in both the study design and survey development phases of the project. Another strength of this study was the low attrition rate, with only six participants dropping out between rounds 1 and 3, which greatly enhanced the interpretability and validity of the findings. Finally, survey element adaptations and suggestions obtained through open-ended questions were diverse in nature with few common items or themes surfacing—a finding that may indicate the breadth and exhaustiveness of the question categories derived from the environmental scan.

This study shares a great deal with others that established consensus on core outcome domains and foci in LC research. It supports many previous findings, while also contributing some novelties. When considering priority LC symptoms to be studied, our study findings share several similarities with Munblit et al’s5 international Delphi study, where researchers established core outcomes for LC; Delphi participants from both studies prioritised symptoms related to fatigue, post-exertional malaise (both physical and cognitive), cardiovascular issues, respiratory problems and cognitive issues (eg, ‘brain fog’). Outcomes related to these same symptoms have also been emphasised in a critical review by Ejalonibu et al,7 who recommended reliable, valid, generalisable and complementary instruments for their measure, and again in a Delphi study on supports for LC,30 where fatigue, respiratory issues and cognitive dysfunction emerged as key foci for rehabilitation treatment. These symptoms have emerged as priority domains of health to be studied in LC.

Differentiating our findings from those of Munblit et al,5 in our study, symptoms related to pain, mental health, the nervous system and gastrointestinal systems were excluded by participants. The number of different pain items (ie, sinus pain, chest pain, rib pain, muscle pain, etc.) included in the survey may have distributed participant responses into separate categories, such that not one emerged as a top priority. It is possible that mental health symptoms were not prioritised due to perceived challenges teasing apart the relationships between LC and anxiety and depression as co-occurring, predictive or secondary symptoms. Parallel to this, some of the open-ended feedback received from participants appeared to indicate that several believed attributing mental health struggles to LC would invalidate the reality of their symptoms being indicative of a true physical disease, and rather, a condition that was ‘all in their head’, similar to existing medical conceptualisations of fibromyalgia and chronic fatigue syndrome.3133 Alternatively, participants may not have prioritised mental health topics as symptoms, given the opportunities to separately rank them as indicators of social and emotional health. Gastrointestinal issues may have received lower priority scores given they carry a shorter duration than some other post-acute LC sequelae.30 Taken together, these findings speak to the diverse nature of LC symptoms and highlight some of the complexities and challenges that affected individuals face in the new reality of their day-to-day lives.

This study makes unique contributions. Unlike Munblit et al’s5 study, where resources (ie, formal healthcare and informal caregiver supports) did not reach a level of consensus, our study saw care topics receive high levels of consensus, even in round 1 of the Delphi. Thus, our study highlights the perceived relevance of gathering data related to formal and informal care, the utilisation of care, quality of care and facilitators and barriers to care. In addition, the quality-of-life indicators prioritised (including measures of social and emotional health and activities of daily living) are a novel contribution, complementing the validated grief measures recommended by Ejalonibu et al,7 and the physical outcomes outlined by Munblit et al.5

Despite its strengths, there are several limitations to this study. First, the representativeness of the panel was limited, with a skew towards women, a lack of ethnic diversity and limitations in the pool from which the patient partners were drawn. Specifically, the patient partners were recruited from the Long COVID Web and may not reflect the same experiences or symptoms as other LC sufferers. As a second limitation, the environmental scan was limited by its search terms and period of coverage (ending in December 2022). As a result of these limitations, the scan did not incorporate findings from the two international Delphi consensus studies examining core outcome sets and core outcome measurement sets. Nevertheless, the outcomes identified by these studies were well-represented in the environmental scan (ie, by other papers). As a third limitation, the study did not include focus groups between rounds of online surveys. This may have limited the number of new ideas or modifications generated. As a fourth limitation, the target sample size was not reached. Although there is no standard sample size for Delphi studies, a larger panel would be desirable to support fuller representation from each group and to increase the likelihood of comprehensive, generalisable results.23 34 Finally, the advisory group was weighted towards researchers, with patient partners and healthcare providers making up a minority. The weighting scheme decided by the advisory group is likely to have reflected this group’s interests more fully than the interests of other groups.

Despite these limitations, the minimum dataset stands as an important first step in establishing cross-jurisdictional LC datasets to improve the understanding and care of patients with LC. Information concerning symptoms, outcomes and care access barriers and facilitators will further progress toward Canada’s Post-COVID-19 Condition Task Force’s recommendation for equitable care pathways for LC.35

ack.title: Acknowledgements
ack.p: The authors wish to acknowledge the Saskatchewan Centre for Patient-Oriented Research and the Long COVID Web for their role in supporting the completion of this research project. We would also like to thank our patient partners Anne Bhéreur, Donna Ellen Frederick and Cindy Mclean for their contributions to the project; and acknowledge Pawan Kumar (Saskatchewan Health Research Foundation) and Alaa Aburub (Saskatchewan Health Quality Council) for their contributions towards the initial conceptualisation of this study; Sarah Slagerman (George & Fay Yee Centre for Healthcare Innovation) for her assistance with the environmental scan; Renee Greene (Saskatchewan Centre for Patient-Oriented Research), Tara Brewer (Digital Health, Saskatchewan Health Authority) and Kristin Dyke (Digital Health, Saskatchewan Health Authority) for their contributions to the synthesis of data following the environmental scan; and Hind Sbihi for their involvement in the Long COVID Web Delphi steering and advisory committee.

fn.p: Funding: This work was supported by the CIHR Canadian Post COVID-19 Condition Research Network Grant #492634, the Saskatchewan Centre for Patient-Oriented Research (SCPOR) and the Long COVID Web Foundational Grant #427761-1084 at the University of Saskatchewan. The Long COVID Web is funded by the Canadian Institutes of Health Research (CIHR)—Grant #185352.
Prepublication history and additional supplemental material for this paper are available online. To view these files, please visit the journal online (https://doi.org/10.1136/bmjopen-2025-111474).
Provenance and peer review: Not commissioned; externally peer reviewed.
Patient consent for publication: Not applicable.

Ethics approval: This study involves human participants and was approved by the University of Saskatchewan Behavioural Research Ethics Board (BEH #4296). Participants gave informed consent to participate in the study before taking part.
Data availability free text: Data is available upon request through the first author.

Collaborators: Long COVID Web Delphi Steering and Advisory Groups: Anne Bhéreur, Caren Cao, Angela Cheung, Simon Décary, Amy Grant, Andrea Gruneir, Gayle Halas, Alison Hoens, Michelle Kho, Grace Lam, Lisa Lix, Kiran Pohar Manhas, Candace McNaughton, Nazeem Muhajarine, Kieran Quinn, Kadia Saint-Onge, Diana Sanchez-Ramirez. Working Group: Adelaide Amah, Bansari Chavda, Donna Dumitrescu, Hammed Ejalonibu, Donna Ellen Frederick, Daphne Kemp, Kathrina Mazurik and Cindy Mclean.

Patient and public involvement: Patients and/or the public were involved in the design, or conduct, or reporting or dissemination plans of this research. Refer to the Methods section for further details.

element-citation.article-title: Post-COVID-19 condition in Canada: what we know, what we don’t know, and a framework for action

sec.title: Data availability statement
sec.p: Data are available upon reasonable request.
